turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor has the opinion this morning in case 16-299, The National Association of Manufacturers v. The Department of Defense. She has asked that I read the opinion for her. The case raises the question what are the waters of the United States? Now as it turns out defining that statutory phrase "a central component of the Clean Water Act" is proven to be a contentious and difficult task. In 2015 the Environmental Protection Agency and the Army Corps of Engineers tried their hand at (inaudible) the definition through an agency regulation dubbed the Waters of the United States Rule or WOTUS Rule. The WOTUS Rule prompted a whirlpool of litigation. Several parties including petitioner National Association of Manufacturers challenged the regulation in federal court. But this case is not about those substantive challenges to the WOTUS Rule. Rather the parties have asked us to wade in on a different question in which federal court must those challenges be filed? There are two principal avenues of judicial review of an action by the EPA. Generally parties may file challenges to final EPA actions in federal district courts ordinarily under the Administrative Procedure Act but the Clean Water Act enumerates seven categories of EPA actions for which review lies directly and exclusively in the federal courts of appeals. The government contends that the WOTUS Rule fits within two of those enumerated categories. Subparagraph E of §1369(b)(1) which covers EPA actions approving or promulgating any effluent limitation or other limitation under §1311, 1312, 1316 or 1345 and subparagraph F of §1369(b)(1) which covers EPA actions issuing or denying any permit under §1342. We disagree because neither of those provisions grants courts of appeals exclusive jurisdiction to review the WOTUS Rule. Any challenges to that rule must be filed in federal district courts. Our conclusion flows directly from the statutory text. To begin, the WOTUS Rule does not fall within subparagraph E which covers any EPA action in approving or promulgating any effluent limitation or other limitation under enumerated sections. The WOTUS Rule is neither an effluent limitation nor another limitation because it does not restrict the discharge of pollutants. Instead it announces a regulatory definition for a statutory term, but even if the WOTUS Rule were such a limitation it nevertheless falls outside the scope of subparagraph E because it is not a limitation promulgated or approved under §1311. As subparagraph E's statutory context makes clear the phrase under §1311 is most naturally read to mean that the effluent or other limitation must be approved or promulgated pursuant to or by reason of the authority of §1311. But the EPA did not promulgate or prove the WOTUS Rule under §1311. Rather the rule was promulgated or approved under §1361(a) which grants the EPA general rulemaking authority. The government fares no better under subparagraph F which grants courts of appeals exclusive and original jurisdiction to review any EPA action "in issuing or denying any permit under §1342." That provision does not cover the WOTUS Rule because the rule does not issue or deny NPDES permits issued under 1342 because the plain language of subparagraph F is unambiguous our inquiry begins with the statutory text and ends there as well. Unable to anchor its preferred reading in the statutory text the government seeks refuge in litany of extra textual considerations that it believes support direct circuit court review of the WOTUS Rule, but those policy considerations alone and in combination do not keep the government's a textual interpretation afloat nor do they provide a convincing basis for us to depart from the statute's plain language. For these and other reasons set forth in our opinion, we reverse the Court of Appeals for the Sixth Circuit and remand with instructions to dismiss the petitions for review for lack of jurisdiction. The decision of the Court is unanimous.
